title: TwoDisjointNode

lean_statement.name: CDMGEquiv
lean_statement.code:
/-- An equivalence between CDMGs `G : CDMG α` and `H : CDMG β` over
potentially distinct carrier types: a bijection `toEquiv : α ≃ β` whose
images on the four data fields `J`, `V`, `E`, `L` of `G` recover the
corresponding fields of `H`. The six prop fields (`disjoint_JV`,
`E_subset`, `L_subset`, `L_irrefl`, `L_symm`, `disjoint_EL`) are
proof-irrelevant once the four data fields are pinned down. -/
structure CDMGEquiv (G : CDMG α) (H : CDMG β) where
  /-- The underlying bijection on the carrier types. -/
  toEquiv : α ≃ β
  /-- The bijection sends `G.J` to `H.J`. -/
  J_eq : H.J = toEquiv '' G.J
  /-- The bijection sends `G.V` to `H.V`. -/
  V_eq : H.V = toEquiv '' G.V
  /-- The bijection sends `G.E` to `H.E` (applied to each endpoint). -/
  E_eq : H.E = (Prod.map toEquiv toEquiv) '' G.E
  /-- The bijection sends `G.L` to `H.L` (applied to each endpoint). -/
  L_eq : H.L = (Prod.map toEquiv toEquiv) '' G.L

lean_statement.name: subset_nodeSplittingOn_V_of_subset_V
lean_statement.code:
-- Helper required to state the row (discharges the inner precondition of the iterated splitting in the fusion lemma's type).
/-- If `W₂ ⊆ G.V`, then `Sum.inl '' W₂` is contained in the vertex set of
the node-split graph `G.nodeSplittingOn W₁ hW₁`. Used to discharge the
inner precondition of the iterated splitting in the fusion lemma below. -/
theorem subset_nodeSplittingOn_V_of_subset_V
    {G : CDMG α} {W₁ W₂ : Set α} (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V) (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.V) :
    Sum.inl '' W₂ ⊆ (G.nodeSplittingOn W₁ hW₁).V := by

lean_statement.name: fusionEquiv
lean_statement.code:
/-- The canonical re-labeling bijection
`α ⊕ ↑(W₁ ∪ W₂) ≃ (α ⊕ ↑W₁) ⊕ ↑(Sum.inl '' W₂)` between the
merged-splitting and iterated-splitting carriers. Built by composing
the Mathlib equivalences `Equiv.Set.union` (disjoint-union of sets),
`Equiv.sumAssoc` (sum reassociation), and `Equiv.Set.image` (transport
along an injective embedding). The disjointness hypothesis
`hdisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂` is consumed by `Equiv.Set.union`; injectivity
of `Sum.inl` is consumed by `Equiv.Set.image`. -/
noncomputable def fusionEquiv (W₁ W₂ : Set α) (hdisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    α ⊕ ↑(W₁ ∪ W₂) ≃
      (α ⊕ ↑W₁) ⊕ ↑((Sum.inl : α → α ⊕ ↑W₁) '' W₂) :=
  letI : DecidablePred (· ∈ W₁) := Classical.decPred _
  (Equiv.sumCongr (Equiv.refl α) (Equiv.Set.union hdisj)).trans <|
    (Equiv.sumAssoc α ↑W₁ ↑W₂).symm.trans <|
      Equiv.sumCongr (Equiv.refl (α ⊕ ↑W₁))
        (Equiv.Set.image (Sum.inl : α → α ⊕ ↑W₁) W₂ Sum.inl_injective)

lean_statement.name: nodeSplittingOn_nodeSplittingOn_equiv
lean_statement.code:
/-- claim_3_7 part 1/2 (fusion lemma): iterated node-splitting is
`CDMGEquiv`-equivalent to a single node-splitting on the union. Mirrors
the first half (`(G_{spl(W₁)})_{spl(W₂)} = G_{spl(W₁ ⊔ W₂)}`) of the
chained equality in the `\Lem` at
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` line 462. -/
noncomputable def nodeSplittingOn_nodeSplittingOn_equiv
    {G : CDMG α} {W₁ W₂ : Set α}
    (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.V) (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V) (hdisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    CDMGEquiv
      ((G.nodeSplittingOn W₁ hW₁).nodeSplittingOn
          (Sum.inl '' W₂)
          (subset_nodeSplittingOn_V_of_subset_V hW₂ hW₁))
      (G.nodeSplittingOn (W₁ ∪ W₂) (Set.union_subset hW₁ hW₂)) := by

lean_statement.name: nodeSplittingOn_comm_equiv
lean_statement.code:
/-- claim_3_7 part 2/2 (commute corollary): swapping `W₁` and `W₂` in
the iterated node-splitting yields a `CDMGEquiv`-equivalent CDMG.
Mirrors the second half
(`(G_{spl(W₁)})_{spl(W₂)} = (G_{spl(W₂)})_{spl(W₁)}`) of the chained
equality in the `\Lem` at `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex`
line 462. -/
noncomputable def nodeSplittingOn_comm_equiv
    {G : CDMG α} {W₁ W₂ : Set α}
    (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.V) (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V) (hdisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    CDMGEquiv
      ((G.nodeSplittingOn W₁ hW₁).nodeSplittingOn
          (Sum.inl '' W₂)
          (subset_nodeSplittingOn_V_of_subset_V hW₂ hW₁))
      ((G.nodeSplittingOn W₂ hW₂).nodeSplittingOn
          (Sum.inl '' W₁)
          (subset_nodeSplittingOn_V_of_subset_V hW₁ hW₂)) := by

lean_explanation:
The LN states a chained equality $(G_{\spl(W_1)})_{\spl(W_2)} = (G_{\spl(W_2)})_{\spl(W_1)} = G_{\spl(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$ for any CDMG $G = (J, V, E, L)$ and any two disjoint subsets $W_1, W_2 \subseteq V$. The Lean encoding splits this single LN equation into two `CDMGEquiv`-valued declarations — one per `=` in the chain — backed by a named canonical bijection `fusionEquiv`.

The ambient vertex type `α : Type u` matches the LN's silent assumption that the graph lives over some carrier; no finiteness instance is needed. `G : CDMG α` is the CDMG; `W₁ W₂ : Set α` are the two subsets, with `hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.V` and `hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V` discharging the LN's $W_1, W_2 \subseteq V$ precondition. `Disjoint W₁ W₂` is Mathlib's order-theoretic disjointness predicate — on sets it amounts to $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, matching the LN's `\dcup`.

The node-splitting construction `G.nodeSplittingOn W h` (from def 3.11) produces a CDMG over carrier `α ⊕ ↑W`: the disjoint sum of `α` (carrying the LN's $0$-copies $v^0$) with the coerced subtype `↑W = {x // x ∈ W}` (carrying the $1$-copies $v^1$). Iterating it once for `W₁` and again for `W₂` yields carrier `(α ⊕ ↑W₁) ⊕ ↑(Sum.inl '' W₂)`; merging the two as a single splitting on `W₁ ∪ W₂` yields carrier `α ⊕ ↑(W₁ ∪ W₂)`. These two carriers are not definitionally equal — precisely why the row encodes the LN's `=` as `CDMGEquiv` rather than literal `Eq`.

`CDMGEquiv G H` is a five-field bundle: a bijection `toEquiv : α ≃ β` on carriers plus four image-equalities `J_eq / V_eq / E_eq / L_eq` capturing that `toEquiv` carries the four data fields of `G` to those of `H` (via Mathlib's `Set.image`, written `'' `, and lifted to pairs by `Prod.map` for the edge fields). `fusionEquiv W₁ W₂ hdisj` is the canonical bijection `α ⊕ ↑(W₁ ∪ W₂) ≃ (α ⊕ ↑W₁) ⊕ ↑(Sum.inl '' W₂)`, composed from `Equiv.Set.union` (disjoint union of sets, consuming `hdisj`), `Equiv.sumAssoc` (associativity of `⊕`), and `Equiv.Set.image` (transport along the injective embedding `Sum.inl`).

`nodeSplittingOn_nodeSplittingOn_equiv` is the fusion direction: it takes `hW₁`, `hW₂`, `hdisj` and produces a `CDMGEquiv` between the iterated splitting `(G.nodeSplittingOn W₁ _).nodeSplittingOn (Sum.inl '' W₂) _` and the merged splitting `G.nodeSplittingOn (W₁ ∪ W₂) _`. `nodeSplittingOn_comm_equiv` is the commute direction: same hypotheses, `CDMGEquiv` between the two iteration orders. In both, `G`, `W₁`, `W₂` are implicit (recovered from the subset hypotheses); the three subset/disjointness hypotheses are explicit because each is part of the LN's stated precondition.
design_choices:
**`CDMGEquiv` rather than literal `Eq` through a pushforward.** The LN reads its chained equality as set-equality after a re-labeling, but the two CDMGs in question live over *different* carrier types — `(α ⊕ ↑W₁) ⊕ ↑(Sum.inl '' W₂)` for the iterated splitting versus `α ⊕ ↑(W₁ ∪ W₂)` for the merged one. Literal `Eq` between them is not even type-correct. `CDMGEquiv` is the direction-symmetric encoding: a bijection on carriers plus four image-equalities on `J / V / E / L`. The runner-up was a `CDMG.relabel : CDMG α → (α ≃ β) → CDMG β` definition, with the row's conclusion stated as `H = G.relabel e`. That shape is more `rw`-friendly but bakes one direction of the bijection into the result and commits `relabel` chapter-wide before there is evidence of what later carrier-shift consumers (SWIG, counterfactual, iSCM) actually want. The conservative encoding is the structure; promoting to a pushforward later — by defining `relabel` once a call-site pattern is visible — is cheaper than the reverse move.

**Four bundled image-equalities, no separate component lemmas and no existential.** Spelling the equivalence out via four top-level lemmas `_J_eq`, `_V_eq`, `_E_eq`, `_L_eq` is unfaithful to the LN, which states *one* displayed equation; downstream consumers want "the two CDMGs are equivalent", not four disconnected component facts they restate per direction of the commute. The opposite extreme — an `∃ e, ...` existential — hides the canonical witness `fusionEquiv` that the commute corollary needs to name when composing two equivalences. The six prop fields of `CDMG` (`disjoint_JV`, `E_subset`, `L_subset`, `L_irrefl`, `L_symm`, `disjoint_EL`) are omitted from `CDMGEquiv`: once the four data-field image-equalities are pinned down, those prop-field obligations on the target CDMG are determined by the source's prop fields plus the bijection. Carrying them inside the bundle adds no information — only opportunity for spurious unification mismatches.

**Fusion and commute as separate declarations, mirroring the LN's own proof structure.** Forré–Mooij display their chained equality $(G_{\spl(W_1)})_{\spl(W_2)} = (G_{\spl(W_2)})_{\spl(W_1)} = G_{\spl(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$ as one equation but factor their proof in the same shape: verify the fusion direction component-wise, then derive the swap "by symmetry". The Lean split therefore mirrors the LN's split. Downstream consumers that want to *collapse* a chained node-splitting reach for `nodeSplittingOn_nodeSplittingOn_equiv`; consumers that want to *reorder* two splittings without collapsing them reach for `nodeSplittingOn_comm_equiv`. A single chained-equality theorem would force one or the other pattern to project, an extra step every time. Three short groupoid laws (`CDMGEquiv.refl`, `.symm`, `.trans`) are shipped alongside the structure so that the commute corollary is a one-line composition of two fusion-lemma invocations after flipping one via `.symm`; a full `CategoryTheory.Groupoid` instance was rejected because it would force `CDMGEquiv` into a `Sigma`-bundled morphism type over the proper class of all CDMGs across all carrier types, with disproportionate universe and size-issue overhead for a one-row corollary.

**`fusionEquiv` built from Mathlib combinators, marked `noncomputable`.** Rolling the four constructor cases of the bijection (and the two round-trip proofs `left_inv` / `right_inv`) by hand would be roughly thirty lines of `dite`-laden case analysis on `Sum.inl a` versus `Sum.inr w` with `w ∈ W₁` versus `w ∈ W₂`. The Mathlib triple `Equiv.Set.union` + `Equiv.sumAssoc` + `Equiv.Set.image` packages the same content with the round-trips already discharged, and exposes `_apply`-shaped `simp` lemmas that let the fusion proof reduce the four image-equality goals via `simp` rather than by unfolding hand-rolled match arms. `Equiv.Set.union` requires `DecidablePred (· ∈ W₁)` to dispatch each $w \in W_1 \cup W_2$ to its $W_1$-or-$W_2$ summand; the instance is supplied via `Classical.decPred`, and the result inherits `noncomputable`. Downstream uses of the fusion lemma are all `Prop`-relevant (in the type of `CDMGEquiv`-valued witnesses), so the classical choice has no observable cost. The codomain wraps in `↑(Sum.inl '' W₂)` rather than the cleaner `↑W₂` because the iterated splitting's second `nodeSplittingOn` call takes that exact `Set` as its argument; avoiding the wrapping would require factoring `nodeSplittingOn` itself to expose a separate carrier-lift helper, which is out of scope for this row.
